question_text: Draw and label the structure of cell membrane. Give the importance of cell membrane protein and lipid. List the organelles of a cell and mention the functions of any three of them.
Mention the functions of cell membrane.
State the functions of mitochondria and endoplasmic reticulum.
Define homeostasis. How is homeostasis maintained in the body?
What do you mean by negative feedback control? Give an example.
Why is a positive feedback mechanism called a vicious cycle?
Classify membrane transport processes.
Discuss energy-dependent transport processes across the cell membrane.
Mention the physiological importance of Na+-K+ pump.
Give the differences between diffusion and osmosis. Give the differences between diffusion and active transport.
Mention the different components of cell membrane.
Give the physiological importance and functions of cell membrane proteins.
Mention the transport processes of micromolecules.
What are the differences between active transport and passive transport?
Why is ECF called the internal environment of our body? Give the ionic composition of ECF.
State the functions of the cell: functions of cell membrane and cell organelles.

Define resting membrane potential. How is the resting membrane potential maintained? Write down the normal range of resting membrane potential in skeletal muscle, smooth muscle, nerve fiber, cardiac muscle, and SA node.
Define membrane potential and action potential.
What is action potential? Draw, label and describe the different stages of action potential in a nerve fiber.
Draw a diagram showing different stages of action potential in a cardiac muscle fibre.
What are the differences between ICF and plasma?
Give the value of Na+, K+, Cl-, HCO3- and Ca++ in plasma.
Give the physiological range of sodium, potassium, calcium and bicarbonate in plasma.
Define osmosis, osmotic pressure and oncotic pressure.
Give the distribution of total body water in a 70 kg male.
Neuromuscular junction and transmission of impulse from nerve ending to the muscle fibre. How is a nerve impulse transmitted through a synapse?
Muscle contraction and relaxation mechanism.
Mention the important functions of Albumin and Globulin.
Mention the important functions of plasma proteins.
Name the plasma proteins with their normal values. Give the value of total plasma protein and the major plasma proteins with function of each type.
What are the sources of plasma proteins?
Write about the composition of blood.
Define erythropoiesis. Mention the different stages of erythropoiesis.
Name the factors that influence erythropoiesis. What are the changes that occur during erythropoiesis?
Outline the steps of Hb synthesis. What are the functions of Hb?
Define anaemia. Classify it. Why does anaemia occur due to Vit-B12 and folic acid deficiency?
Briefly describe the fate of RBC. What is the difference between a mature RBC and immature RBC?
Discuss the role of erythropoietin in erythropoiesis.
Give the value of formed elements of blood in SI unit.
Give the normal value of Hb in adult.
Write about MCV, MCH, MCHC and PCV.
Name the stages of differentiation of red blood cells.
Show the fate of haemoglobin in a figure. Give the normal value of bilirubin in plasma.
Define polycythemia. What are its types?
Define and classify jaundice.
What are the different types of WBC? Describe the properties of WBC. Write down the functions of WBC. Mention the differential count of WBC.
Define leucocytosis and leucopenia.
What is phagocytosis?
Define haemostasis. What are the basic steps of haemostasis?
Briefly describe the extrinsic pathway of blood coagulation.
Mention the name of vitamin K dependent clotting factors.
Why does blood not clot inside the vessels?
What are the basic steps of coagulation? Why does blood not normally clot within circulation?
Write a short note on Haemophilia.
Write a short note on bleeding time and clotting time (BT, CT).
Define haemopoiesis. Mention the site of haemopoiesis. What do you mean by extramedullary haemopoiesis?
What are the ABO blood groups with their agglutinogens and agglutinins?
What is Rh incompatibility? What are the immediate hazards of mismatched blood transfusion?
Mention the importance of blood grouping.
State Landsteiner's law.
Write a short note on Erythroblastosis fetalis.
Name the junctional tissues of the heart. What is AV nodal delay? Why is SA node called the pacemaker of the heart?
Explain autorhythmicity and refractory period of cardiac muscle.
Name the junctional tissues of the heart with their impulse generation rate and conduction velocity.
Mention the properties of cardiac muscle. State All or None law and Frank-Starling law with their importance.
What is the All or None law and Frank-Starling law? Why cannot cardiac muscle be tetanized?
Tell the properties of cardiac muscle. State the refractory period with its types and importance.
Name the properties of cardiac muscle. State the Frank-Starling law and All or None law of cardiac muscle.
State the properties of cardiac muscles. Tell the rhythmicity of SA node and Purkinje fiber.
Name some vasoconstrictor and vasodilator agents. Explain the role of the autonomic nervous system on the heart.
Define cardiac cycle and cardiac cycle time. What are the events of cardiac cycle? Calculate the cardiac cycle time if HR is 60 beats/min.
What are the changes that occur in different events of cardiac cycle?
Name the events of ventricular systole and diastole. Mention left ventricular pressure and volume changes during isometric contraction and relaxation phases.
Tell the phases of ventricular systole. In which phase is intraventricular pressure sharply raised and why? What is refractory period? Tell its types and importance.
What are phases of ventricular diastole? In which phase is 2nd heart sound produced and why?
Define heart sound. What are the types of heart sound? What is the physiological basis of heart sound? Tell the importance of 2nd heart sound. Why is 2nd heart sound louder than 1st heart sound?
What are the heart sounds produced during ventricular diastole? Tell their causes.
Define ECG. Tell the components of normal ECG with their interpretation. Tell the importance of R-R interval and PR interval.
Mention the importance of ECG.
Define and classify heart block. What is sinus arrhythmia? What is the importance of PR interval?
If a person's cardiac rhythm is regular and number of large squares between R-R interval is 5 in ECG tracing, what will be the heart rate?
An athlete comes with an ECG which reveals heart rate 55 b/min. What is your impression? What are the other causes of this condition?

A patient admitted with history of dizziness and fainting attacks. ECG shows no relationship between P waves and QRS complexes. What type of heart block is it? What is the term used for the fainting attack?
What are the phases of action potential of cardiac muscle with its causes?
What is AV nodal delay? Tell its cause and importance.
State the functional classification of blood vessels with examples. What is microcirculation?
Which vessel is the seat for peripheral resistance and why?
Name some vasoconstrictor agents. Tell their role.
Define cardiac output with its normal value and equation. Name the factors controlling cardiac output.
Define SV and EDV. If EDV is 130 ml and SV is 80 ml, what will be the ESV?
Define EDV and ESV with their normal values. If EDV is 130 ml and ESV is 50 ml, calculate the stroke volume.
A man consumes 200 ml of oxygen per minute. His arterial oxygen content is 200 ml/L and the oxygen content of mixed venous blood is 160 ml/L. What will be his cardiac output?
Define venous return with its normal value. What are the factors affecting venous return?
Define pulse with its normal range in adult. What are the points to be noted during examination of radial pulse? Define and classify pulse.
Define stroke volume with its normal value. If stroke volume is 70 ml and HR is 100 beats/min, what will be the cardiac output?
If HR is 80 beats/min and SV is 70 ml/beat, what will be the cardiac output?
Define heart rate with its normal value. What are the causes of tachycardia?
Define peripheral resistance. What are the factors affecting peripheral resistance?
Define blood pressure. Tell the types of blood pressure with their normal values. Tell the importance of mean arterial pressure.
Tell about the baroreceptor feedback mechanism of blood pressure control. Name the baroreceptors with their location.
Tell about the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone mechanism of blood pressure regulation.
How is blood pressure regulated by the chemoreceptor feedback mechanism?
A 40 years male patient presented with vertigo. On measurement his blood pressure was 140/100 mmHg. What is your impression? What are the causes of secondary hypertension?
A young man has blood pressure 120/60 mmHg. What will be his pulse pressure and mean arterial pressure?
Calculate the pulse pressure and mean pressure if systolic BP is 140 mmHg and diastolic BP is 80 mmHg.
A person brought to emergency following a road accident. Skin is cold, clammy and BP 70/50 mmHg. Tell the diagnosis and other features of this condition.
Describe the circulatory adjustments during muscular exercise.
Give the causes of tachycardia and bradycardia. Why do athletes have a low heart rate?
A person came with complaints of palpitation and heart rate was 110 b/min. What is the name of this condition? Mention its causes.
If the heart rate is 50 beats/min, mention the probable condition. Tell its causes.
Define shock. Classify shock with examples. Tell the physiological compensatory mechanism activated by hypovolemic shock.
Define preload and afterload. Tell the effects of severe exercise on blood pressure and heart rate.
What are the differences between systemic circulation and pulmonary circulation?
Define respiration. Distinguish between inspiration and expiration.
Name the muscles of inspiration and expiration.
Define pulmonary ventilation and alveolar ventilation. Write down their formulas and normal values.
Define and give the normal values of: TV, IRV, ERV, RV.
Define and give the normal values of: VC, FRC, TLC, IC.
Define compliance of lung. What are the factors that determine lung compliance?
Define surfactant. Mention its source, composition and functions. What is respiratory distress syndrome?
Define dead space. Differentiate between anatomical and physiological dead space.
Name some lung function tests. What is the significance of FEV1/FVC ratio?
Give the composition of atmospheric air and alveolar air.
Define respiratory unit. Name the structures of respiratory membrane.
Name the factors that affect the diffusion of gases through the respiratory membrane.
Mention the peculiarities of pulmonary circulation.
Define ventilation-perfusion ratio (V/Q). What is the normal value? What happens if V/Q is zero or infinity?
How is oxygen transported in the blood?
How is carbon dioxide transported in the blood?
Draw and label the oxy-hemoglobin dissociation curve. Name the factors that shift the curve to the right.
What is Bohr effect? Tell its significance.
What is Haldane effect? Tell its significance.
Explain chloride shift (Hamburger phenomenon) during CO2 transport.
Name and locate the respiratory centers in the brainstem.
Explain the chemical regulation of respiration (role of central and peripheral chemoreceptors).
Describe the Hering-Breuer inflation reflex and its significance.
Define hypoxia. Classify hypoxia with one example of each type.
Differentiate between anemic hypoxia and hypoxic hypoxia.
Define cyanosis. Mention its types and causes.
Define dyspnea, hypercapnia, and apnea.
What is periodic breathing? Give an example (Cheyne-Stokes breathing).
Name the local hormones of GIT. Mention the sources and functions of cholecystokinin, motilin, gastrin, and secretin.
Tell about the enteric nervous system: types, locations, functions. Define ENS. State the location and functions of Myenteric and Meissner's plexus.
What are the stimuli for secretion, site of secretion and functions of secretin and cholecystokinin?
State the functions of small intestine and colon. What is gastrocolic reflex? Why is it more potent in children?
Tell the extrinsic neural control of GIT.
Why are GIT hormones called so? Tell the functions of any one of them.
List the movements of large intestine. Describe any one of them. Tell the mechanism and purpose of haustration.
List the movements of the alimentary tract according to location. Tell about segmentation contraction: mechanism and purpose.
Name the gastrointestinal reflexes. What do you mean by defecation reflex?
What are the basic movements of GIT? What are the purpose and mechanism of peristalsis?
What are the motor functions of stomach? What are the factors affecting stomach emptying?
What are the functions of stomach? What is the law of gut? Which condition does not follow the law of gut?
What is gastric emptying? Tell the factors affecting gastric emptying.
Tell the secretory function of stomach. What is gastrocolic reflex?
Name the movements of small intestine. State the mechanism of any one of them.
A person attended OPD with history of passage of loose watery stool 6 times/day. What is your probable diagnosis? Which type of GIT movement is involved?
Define swallowing. What are the stages of it? In which phase does physiological apnoea occur?
Define law of gut. Compare the functions of cholecystokinin and secretin on pancreas.
Tell the functions of stomach. Name the cells present in stomach with their functions.
What is peristaltic rush?
Define and classify nephron. Tell the differences between cortical and juxtamedullary nephron.
What are the functions of the kidney?
What are the structures of the glomerular membrane? Why cannot protein pass through the glomerular membrane? Why is glomerular capillary more permeable than other capillaries?
Define nephron. What are the parts of nephron? What is a juxtaglomerular apparatus and what are its components and functions?
Mention the peculiarities of renal circulation.
Name the hormones acting on the kidney with their functions.
Differentiate between glomerular capillary and peritubular capillary.
Name the hormones secreted from the kidney. Tell the homeostatic functions of the kidney.
Tell the value of renal blood flow and renal plasma flow.
Define GFR with its normal value. Calculate net filtration pressure. What are the forces favoring and opposing glomerular filtration?
Name the basic steps of urine formation. How does glucose reabsorb in different parts of the renal tubule?
How is GFR autoregulated by tubuloglomerular feedback mechanism and afferent arteriolar vasodilator feedback mechanism?
What are the factors that affect GFR?
What is glomerular filtrate? State the differences between glomerular filtrate and plasma.
Why is inulin used to measure GFR?
What are the dynamics of GFR? How do you calculate net filtration pressure?
Name the substances completely reabsorbed in the proximal convoluted tubule. Explain how glucose is reabsorbed in the renal tubule.
Mention the functions of the proximal convoluted tubule. How is Na+ reabsorbed in the ascending limb of Henle?
Name the hormones acting on the kidney and mention their functions in the renal tubule.
Name the substances secreted by the renal tubule. How does amino acid reabsorb in the renal tubule?
What is plasma clearance? State the plasma clearance values of creatinine, urea and inulin. Tell the importance of plasma clearance in assessment of renal function.
Differentiate between water diuresis and osmotic diuresis. Define diuresis and classify it with causes.
What is renal threshold? What is the normal threshold for glucose? What is the normal value of TmG?
What is plasma load and tubular load? Tell the normal values for glucose.
What is obligatory urine volume? Mention its normal value. Define oliguria, polyuria, anuria.
How is bicarbonate reabsorbed from the renal tubules?
How is water reabsorbed in the different parts of the renal tubules? What is facultative reabsorption of water? Mention the role of ADH in water reabsorption.
How is H+ secreted in the renal tubule? Name the substances secreted by PCT.
Tell the functions of late DCT.
Tell the osmolarity of concentrated and dilute urine. Name the factors on which formation of concentrated urine depends. What are the prerequisites for formation of concentrated urine?
What is the countercurrent mechanism? Name the factors that cause hyperosmotic renal medullary interstitium.
Tell the components of the countercurrent mechanism. Why does vasa recta act as a countercurrent exchanger?
What are the homeostatic functions of the kidney?
Name the basic steps of urine formation. What are the secreted substances present in urine? What are the functions of proximal tubules?
What are the abnormalities of micturition? Mention their causes.
A 45-year-old male patient presented with complaints of polyuria. Fasting blood glucose level was 10.8 mmol/L. What is your probable diagnosis? Mention the other features.
A patient came to OPD with history of frequency of micturition and daily urine output of 3.5 L/day. What is the probable diagnosis? Tell the different types of this condition with examples.
Urine RME of a patient revealed proteinuria and presence of RBC in urine. Is it normal? Why do RBC and protein not appear in normal urine?

A 35-year-old male presented with dribbling of urine after road traffic accident with injury to sacral region of spinal cord. Tell the probable diagnosis and cause. Mention the other abnormalities of micturition.
What is a hormone? Classify hormones according to their chemical nature with one example. Classify on the basis of location of receptor with example.
Name steroid hormones. Explain the mechanism of action of steroid hormones.
Tell the location of hormone receptors with examples. How does a steroid hormone act?
Classify hormones on the basis of site of action with examples.
Name some hormones that act through cAMP second messenger systems. Where is their receptor present?
What do you mean by tropic hormones? Name some tropic hormones with their source and site of action.
Define hormone. Tell the outline of mechanism of action of different types of hormones.
Name the hormones of the anterior pituitary gland. How is anterior pituitary secretion regulated by the hypothalamus?
Name the hypothalamic releasing and inhibitory hormones.
Name the posterior pituitary hormones. Where are they synthesized? Explain the physiological effects of each.
Mention the function of ADH. Why is ADH called vasopressin? How does it act as an antidiuretic hormone?
Name the anterior pituitary cells with their secretions. Enumerate the effects of growth hormone on carbohydrate and protein metabolism. What are the diabetogenic effects of growth hormone?
State the functions of growth hormone on bone and cartilage. Tell the non-metabolic functions of growth hormone.
What are the features that occur due to deficiency of GH in children? Tell the types of this condition.
Give the differences between acromegaly and gigantism.
Tell about milk ejection reflex. Explain the name of posterior pituitary hormones.
A 50-year-old person presented with hyperglycemia, increased shoe size and protrusion of lower jaw. What may be the diagnosis? Tell the other clinical features.
A child presented with short stature for age. Name two hormonal disorders that can cause this. How can you differentiate between them?
A 15-year-old boy presented with short stature but proportionate growth and normal intelligence. Which hormonal disorder do you suspect? What are the types?
A 35-year-old man presented with enlarged hands, feet and kyphosis. What is the probable diagnosis, cause and other clinical features?
Name the hormones secreted from the thyroid gland. Name the steps of biosynthesis of thyroid hormone. How will you differentiate between T3 and T4?
Tell the functions of thyroid hormone on CVS and brain. Tell the function of thyroid hormone on growth.
How is thyroid hormone secretion regulated? Tell the role of TSH in thyroid hormone synthesis.
Which thyroid hormone is more potent and why?
What is cretinism and myxedema? Differentiate between cretinism and dwarfism.
Give the clinical features of hypothyroidism.
A 40-year-old female patient presented with protrusion of eyeball, weight loss and muscle weakness. What is your probable diagnosis? How can you diagnose by laboratory investigation?
A 40-year female patient presented with puffy face and bagginess under eye. What is your diagnosis? How can you diagnose by laboratory investigation?
A 35-year female patient presented with history of tiredness, constipation and weight gain. What is your probable diagnosis? Mention the other features.
A 7-year-old child attended OPD with stunted growth and dribbling of saliva. What is your probable diagnosis? Tell the cause and other features.
A 32-year female presented with exophthalmos; investigation revealed plasma FT3, FT4 increased and plasma TSH decreased. Tell the probable diagnosis and other clinical features.
A 3-year-old boy presented with poor physical and mental growth. Tell the probable diagnosis, cause and one important test.
Name the hormone regulating blood calcium level. How is blood calcium level maintained in our body?
What are the functions of ionized calcium? What are the hormones responsible for calcium homeostasis?
Explain why tetany occurs. Mention the clinical features of tetany.
Tell the forms of Ca2+ present in plasma. Tell the normal Ca2+ level in blood.
Tell the number of parathyroid glands with their location. Mention the functions of PTH.

A 35-year-old female presented with cramps and stiffness in the leg, tingling sensation around the mouth. Serum calcium was 5.8 mg/dl. What is your probable diagnosis? What are the other clinical features?
A 20-year female presented with carpopedal spasm and plasma calcium level 4 mg/dl. Tell the probable diagnosis with other features.
Name the layers of adrenal cortex with their hormones. State the functions of aldosterone on the kidney. Tell the mechanism of action of aldosterone.
How is aldosterone secretion regulated? What is aldosterone escape? Why is aldosterone called 'life-saving hormone'?
State the anti-inflammatory action and metabolic functions of cortisol. Outline the functions of cortisol.
Name the hormones of the adrenal gland. Tell the role of cortisol in inflammation.
What condition arises due to hypersecretion of the middle layer of adrenal cortex?
A 40-year-old female presented with moon face and buffalo-like torso. Tell the probable diagnosis, cause and other clinical features.
Name the hormones that regulate blood glucose level. What are the actions of insulin in our body?
Name the cells of Islets of Langerhans with their hormones. What are the consequences of insulin deficiency on carbohydrate, protein and fat metabolism?
What are the actions of glucagon in our body?
What is hyperglycemia? Name the condition that arises due to deficiency of insulin. Why is insulin called the hormone of abundance? How does insulin act?
Tell the functions of insulin on carbohydrate, protein and fat metabolism.

A 40-year-old male presented with increased appetite, increased thirst and increased urine output. What is your probable diagnosis? Which hormone is affected? Name the other hormones involved in regulation of blood glucose.
A 34-year-old male presented with excessive thirst; fasting blood glucose was 7.6 mmol/L. Tell the probable diagnosis and other clinical features.
Tell the metabolic function of glucagon.
Define spermatogenesis. Mention the steps and hormones responsible for spermatogenesis.
What are the functions of the testis? Mention the secondary sexual characteristics of male.
Define sex differentiation and sex determination. What are the functions of gonads?
Define puberty. What are the changes observed in females during puberty? Mention the events of puberty in females.
Name the primary sex organs of male with their functions. What are the secondary sexual characteristics in male?
Name the sex hormones of male. State the functions of Leydig cells and Sertoli cells.
Name the sex hormones. State the functions of testosterone in fetal life and in adult male.
Name the gonadotropins. Mention the functions of gonadotropins in male.
Tell the normal sperm count. What are the changes that occur during the maturation of sperm (spermiogenesis)?
Tell the age of puberty in boys and girls. What are the secondary sex characteristics of female?
Define menarche, amenorrhea, and menopause.
A 40-year-old male presented with enlarged hands, feet and protrusion of lower jaw. What is your probable diagnosis? Tell the cause and other features.
What is corpus luteum? When does the degeneration of corpus luteum occur? Explain its fate and function.
Calculate the probable day of ovulation in a 32-day cycle. State the mechanism of ovulation.
What are the phases of the menstrual cycle? Mention the hormones that control the various phases of the menstrual cycle.
What is the ovarian cycle? State the different phases of the ovarian cycle. In which phase is ovum discharged?
Define ovulation. Explain the mechanism of ovulation. What are the indicators of ovulation?
Define menstrual cycle. Explain the phases of the menstrual cycle with responsible hormones.
What are the female sex hormones? State the functions of oestrogen and progesterone on the uterus and breast.
What are the changes that occur during the proliferative phase of the menstrual cycle? Duration of which phase is fixed?
What is LH surge? Tell the cause and importance of LH surge.
What is an anovulatory cycle? How is it produced?
A female 20-year-old came with history of menstrual cycle of 34 days. Is it a normal cycle? What was the probable ovulation day?
A woman with a regular menstrual cycle of 30 days. Calculate her probable date of ovulation. Tell 3 indicators of ovulation.
A 25-year female presented with absence of menstrual bleeding for 6 months but had previously normal menstrual periods. What is your probable diagnosis? What are the causes of this condition?
How can you prevent ovulation in a normal menstruating woman?
Name the hormones that act on breast development with their functions. Tell the role of prolactin and oxytocin during lactation.
Name the placental hormones with their functions. Mention the functions of hCG for maintaining pregnancy.
Define lactation. Tell the pathway of milk ejection reflex. What is lactational amenorrhoea?
Define mammogenesis and lactogenesis. Mention the physiological effects of oxytocin on the gravid uterus and breast.
What is contraception? What are the types of contraceptives? Name permanent methods of contraception in male and females. Tell the mechanism of the classical pill.
Tell the sources and functions of progesterone.
Define synapse. Mention the parts of a synapse. How are neurotransmitters released from the presynaptic terminal?
Tell the functional classification of synapse. Name the components of synapse. Tell the properties of synapse.
Define EPSP and IPSP with their causes.
How is a nerve impulse transmitted through a chemical synapse? What do you mean by synaptic delay?
Define and classify neurons according to the number of processes with one example. Differentiate between axon and dendrite.
Define neuron. Classify neurons with example.
Define neurotransmitter. Classify neurotransmitters according to rapidity of action and according to function with examples.
Define nerve fiber. Classify nerve fiber on the basis of diameter and conduction velocity. What are the properties of a nerve fiber?
Name the properties of nerve fiber. How does an impulse conduct through a myelinated nerve fiber?
What do you mean by fast and slow pain with one example of each? Which tract carries pain sensation?
Name the ascending tracts. Which tract carries fine touch and tactile localization from the lower half of the body? Tell its origin and termination.
Name the general senses with their receptors. Tell the sensations that pass through the dorsal column-medial lemniscal system.
Classify sensory receptors according to their nature of stimuli. Give one example of each. Define sensory receptor.
Name the ascending tracts. What is Bell-Magendie Law?
Name the special senses with their organs and receptors.
Define tract. Mention the functions of the Tract of Goll and Tract of Burdach. Tell the origin, termination and functions of the spinothalamic tract.
Mention the functions of the lateral spinothalamic tract. Tell the pain pathway.
Name and functions of Brodmann's areas of the cerebral cortex.
How is fine touch sensation transmitted from periphery to centre?
Define and classify reflex with examples. Tell the properties of reflex action. Tell the reflex arc of the knee jerk and its clinical importance.
Define and classify reflex arc. Tell the components of reflex arc.
Define and classify light reflex. Explain the reflex arc of light reflex. What is the cause of consensual light reflex?
What do you mean by release phenomenon? Give the importance of knee jerk. If the knee jerk is pendular, what may be the site of lesion?
How is knee jerk elicited? Tell its clinical importance.
Define muscle tone. What are its functions? How is it maintained? Define muscle spindle and Golgi tendon organ with their physiological anatomy and functions.
Define UMN and LMN with examples. What is Babinski's sign?
How does a lesion of UMN differ from LMN? Differentiate between UMNL and LMNL.
Mention the origin, function and termination of the pyramidal tract (corticospinal tract).
Name the extrapyramidal tracts. What are the functions of the extrapyramidal tract?
What are the features of LMNL? What is release phenomenon?
What changes in the pattern of deep reflexes do you expect in UMNL? What are the other features of UMNL?
Tell the effects of lesion below the level of hemisection of spinal cord.
A 65-year-old male presented with flaccid type of muscle paralysis. What is your probable diagnosis? Mention the other features.
A 60-year-old male presented with spastic type paralysis with increased muscle tone. What is your probable diagnosis? Mention the other features.

A 50-year-old male presented with sudden onset of inability to move his right upper and lower limbs with Babinski's sign positive and increased muscle tone. What is your probable diagnosis? Which part of the nervous system is affected? What changes in deep reflexes do you expect and why?

A 40-year-old man presented with sudden onset of inability to move right upper and lower limbs with spastic paralysis on the right side. What is your probable diagnosis? What will be the other features?
State the functional divisions of cerebellum with their functions. What is dysdiadochokinesia?
Tell some features of cerebellar lesion.
A 45-year-old male presented with involuntary jerky movement of eyeball and intention tremor. Tell the probable diagnosis, three clinical features and tests to be done.
Name the functional parts of basal ganglia. Tell some features of basal ganglia lesion.
Tell the functional components and functions of thalamus, reticular formation, and limbic system.
A 60-year-old male presented with intention tremor; gait deviates to the affected side when walking. Tell the probable diagnosis, cause and other clinical features.
Tell the vegetative functions of hypothalamus. Name the nuclei of hypothalamus.
Explain how body temperature is regulated in a hot climate. How body temperature is regulated in cold climate.
Tell the processes of heat loss from our body with their percentage.
Which nucleus of hypothalamus is stimulated when body temperature is raised?
Tell the normal body temperature. What are the processes of heat gain of the body?
Define core temperature and shell temperature.
How does the hypothalamus regulate body fluid osmolarity?
Explain the physiological anatomy of the sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous system and their functions.
Describe the alarm or stress response (fight or flight response).
What are the effects of injury if lesion occurs at optic nerve, crossed and uncrossed fibers of optic chiasma, optic tract, and optic radiation?
Define accommodation reaction. List the changes that occur during accommodation and their advantages.
If a 17-year-old girl comes with difficulty in seeing distant objects, what may be her error of refraction? Why does this problem arise and how can it be corrected?
What do you mean by near point and far point? What is dark adaptation? What are the changes that occur in dark adaptation?
Which is the common refractive error for the age group 40-45 years? What is the cause and how can it be corrected?
Name the photoreceptors with their functions. What is rhodopsin? Differentiate between rods and cones.
State the visual pathway. Tell the effect of lesion of crossed fibers of the visual pathway.
Name the refractive media of eye with their refractive index. Mention the nervous layers of the retina.
If bright light is suddenly focused on one eye, why does the pupil of the other eye constrict? Tell the light reflex pathway.
Tell the functions of cones. What is visual acuity? Where is it maximum? What is its importance?
Define and classify light reflex. Tell the reflex arc of light reflex. What is Argyll-Robertson pupil and its causes?
What is light adaptation? What are the changes that occur during light adaptation?
Define binocular vision with its advantages. What is the far point of vision?
Name the primary colors. What do you mean by color vision?
What type of visual field defect occurs due to lesion of the left optic nerve? Right optic nerve? Right optic tract?
Name the common errors of refraction. Mention the cause and correction of any one of them.
A 13-year-old boy presented with difficulty to see distant objects clearly. Tell the probable diagnosis, cause and how this condition can be corrected.
A 30-year female presented with difficulty in seeing near objects clearly. Tell the probable diagnosis, cause and how it can be corrected.

A 35-year-old man presented with difficulty in seeing objects on either side of his visual field; hands and feet were very large. Tell the probable diagnosis and type of visual field defect and site of lesion.
When someone's hearing diminishes or is lost, what may this condition be called? What are the types and how will you differentiate among them?
Define and classify deafness. Mention the causes of any one type.
Tell the causes of nerve deafness. What are the tests to diagnose conductive and nerve deafness?
If Rinne's test is positive, what will be your inference? Mention the name of the tests of hearing with their interpretation.
A patient came with complaints of impaired hearing. Vibratory sound in air is not heard after bone conduction is over. Which type of deafness is it? Mention the causes.
Name the receptor for hearing. Describe the mechanism of hearing and sound transmission and the auditory pathway.
Name the special senses with their receptors. What are the modalities of taste sensation with location of respective taste buds? What is Anosmia?
Name the nerve that carries taste sensation from the tongue. Where does taste sensation terminate? How is taste sensation transmitted from the tongue to the cerebral cortex?
What are the primary sensations of taste? Name the locations of the taste buds.
Tell the modalities of taste sensation with their causative agents. What are the abnormalities of taste sensation? What is Ageusia?
Tell the smell receptor and pathway.
